name: B.A. in Digital Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan progresses logically from Art & Design foundations (AD 150 Color and Composition, AD 111/112 traditional and digital media, AD 161->162 history) into the core Digital Design studio ladder DD 263 -> 264 -> 363 -> 364 and on to the AD 463 Collaborative Design Studio capstone (deepestPrereqChain 5). Specialization courses (DD 275 History of Games, DD 301 Acting for Animators, DD 303/403 Sound and Music, DD 334 Simulated Environments, DD 443/444 Character Design, IT 265 Game Architecture) slot in at sensible points. The main gap is that only 7 requirement groups parsed, so the Fourth-Year second semester is missing from the visible progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the missing Fourth-Year second-semester term so the studio sequence resolves cleanly to graduation and the capstone's placement is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well developed and mostly clean (deepestPrereqChain 5), with the DD studio chain properly gated (DD 264 requires AD 111, AD 112, AD 150 and DD 263; DD 364 requires ARCH 382, DD 275, DD 363, IT 201). missingCourseCount is low at 6, and the danglers are cross-listed or placeholder items: ARCH 251/282/334, FA 264, GER 300, and the PHYS 102A lab. Some prerequisite strings are, however, convoluted (DD 275's 'AD 111, AD 112 and AD 162 or ARCH 163, ARCH 263 and ARCH 251').
dimensions.suggestion: Simplify and parenthesize the tangled prerequisite strings (notably DD 275) and register PHYS 102A and the cross-listed ARCH/FA course codes so the 6 dangling references resolve.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only the plan of study and course list are present. For a design B.A. spanning games, animation, motion, and interactive media, an explicit outcomes statement is needed to define the shared competency across those tracks.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering visual design fundamentals, digital 2D/3D production, interactive/motion media, and collaborative studio practice.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is null because statedTotalCredits is null, so the plan cannot be reconciled to a target; this is a data-consistency caveat to note rather than a defect. The summed 106 credits across the 7 parsed terms sit in a reasonable 14-17 credits-per-term band, but with the total unstated and one term missing, the gen-ed/major/elective balance cannot be fully verified.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the program's total required credits (e.g., 120-124) and the missing term so the plan reconciles and the general-education vs. studio-core vs. elective split is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The seven parsed terms are labeled with per-term credits and the studio spine is legible, but the page carries extraction artifacts: multiple 'or ...' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows (MATH 107 'or MATH 113', CS 100 'or CS 115', PSY 210 'or R830 101', IT 265 'or IT 266', DD 443 'or DD 444'), the Fourth-Year second semester is absent, and no program description or total-credit figure is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Fold each 'or' alternative into its parent course row, add the missing term and a program overview, and state the total credits so the page reads as a complete plan.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is strong for games, animation, and motion media (DD 263-364 studios, DD 275 History of Games, DD 443/444 character design, DD 303/403 sound, IT 265 Game Architecture, AD 201 Human Factors). Currency gaps stand out, though: DD 334 Simulated Environments is described as 'desktop, non-immersive virtual reality,' which is dated; there is no named real-time game engine (Unity/Unreal), no dedicated UX/UI or interaction-design course, and no coverage of generative-AI tooling now central to digital media.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize DD 334 toward real-time engines and immersive XR, and add explicit UX/UI/interaction-design and generative-AI-tooling courses.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are generally rich and specific, naming concrete media and methods (DD 264 covers motion graphics and time-based media; DD 443 covers character sheets and creature design for 2D games; DD 334 describes non-immersive VR for representing architecture). Many carry useful 'Restrictions: For Digital Design majors only' registration notes. Only excerpts are truncated; few read as thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and sensible (DD 264 <- AD 111/112/150 and DD 263; AD 463 <- (DD 364 or ID 364 or INT 364 or ARCH 364) and PHYS 102). Clarity suffers from a few genuinely tangled, unparenthesized strings such as DD 275's 'AD 111, AD 112 and AD 162 or ARCH 163, ARCH 263 and ARCH 251', where the intended logic is ambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: The program teaches game architecture (IT 265) and 3D studios but never names a real-time engine; contemporary games/interactive work is engine-centric and this is the largest practical gap.
AD 201 Human Factors and IT 201 Information Design touch usability, but no course covers interface/interaction design, a core employment path for digital-design graduates.
Character work (DD 443/444) and 3D studios exist, yet there is no technical-art course on shaders, lighting, or real-time materials that bridges art and engine pipelines.
IT 201 Information Design is the only web-adjacent course; interactive media careers expect front-end/creative-coding skills beyond it.

missingTopics.topic: Motion capture and character animation pipeline
missingTopics.rationale: DD 301 Acting for Animators and DD 444 3D character development imply performance-driven animation but no course covers the mocap-to-rig production pipeline.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI for image, video, and 3D
emergingTrends.rationale: Generative tools are rapidly reshaping concept art, texturing, and animation; the studio and character courses (DD 363/364, DD 443/444) are natural places to integrate them.
Engine-based virtual production is now standard in film and games; modernizing DD 334 toward real-time pipelines would align the program with industry.
DD 334's 'non-immersive, desktop VR' framing is dated; immersive XR is a major growth area the simulated-environments course should be updated to cover.
Procedural and data-driven asset creation is expanding in games and motion media, a skill the current 3D studios could incorporate.
Interactive installations and web experiences are a leading destination for digital designers, arguing for a dedicated creative-coding/interaction course.

topSuggestions: Add a real-time game-engine course (Unity/Unreal) and modernize DD 334 from desktop non-immersive VR toward immersive XR and real-time pipelines.
Add a dedicated UX/UI and interaction-design course to serve the interactive-media career path the current plan underserves.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes and the program's total required credits so the plan reconciles (statedTotalCredits is currently null).
Integrate generative-AI tooling into the studio and character courses (DD 363/364, DD 443/444) to keep production skills current.
Clean the plan-of-study parse: fold the 'or' alternatives (CS 100/CS 115, MATH 107/113, PSY 210/R830 101, DD 443/444) into their parent rows and add the missing Fourth-Year second semester.
